contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Hashing typically buys non-mutating expected lookup with O(n) auxiliary state; sorting may reduce auxiliary state only when mutation is legal and the chosen sort's memory contract is explicit.

items.prompt: The input array must remain byte-for-byte unchanged, and the task needs one-pass duplicate detection. Which tradeoff is most relevant?

options.text: A hash set can preserve input order and contents at the cost of O(k) auxiliary entries.
Sort the original array in place because lower memory overrides the immutability contract.
Choose solely from headline time complexity.
Compare only adjacent original elements.

feedback.reason: The strategy must first satisfy the non-mutation contract.
feedback.details: Hashing provides equality state without reordering the input, but its memory cost must be accepted explicitly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_in_place: Mutating the input would be incorrect.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_contract: Mutation is an observable correctness constraint.

items.prompt: The input may be reordered, memory is tightly constrained, and the result only asks whether any duplicate exists. Which option can reduce auxiliary memory?

options.text: Sort in place and scan adjacent values.
Build a hash set and claim constant auxiliary space.
Copy the array and sort the copy.
Use all pairs because it allocates no table.

feedback.reason: When mutation is legal, an in-place sorting path can trade O(n log n) time for much lower auxiliary memory.
feedback.details: The exact sort algorithm's stack or buffer contract still needs to be stated.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_set: The set may contain O(n) entries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.copy_sort: The copy adds O(n) auxiliary storage.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pair_check: O(n²) time may be infeasible and is not the intended hash-versus-sort tradeoff.

items.prompt: Why is the statement "sorting uses O(1) extra space" incomplete?

options.text: Auxiliary space depends on the chosen sort, stability requirement, recursion stack, and whether a copy is needed.
Sorting never uses memory.
Hashing and sorting always use identical memory.
Only output memory can ever matter.

feedback.reason: Sorting is a family, not one universal memory profile.
feedback.details: An in-place introsort may use O(log n) stack, merge sort commonly uses O(n) buffer, and preserving the original input may require an O(n) copy.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_never_memory: Many sorts use stacks or buffers.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_only: Auxiliary working memory is part of the strategy cost.

items.prompt: A hash frequency table processes n values with k distinct keys. What normally drives its auxiliary-space growth?

options.text: The number of stored distinct keys and associated metadata, O(k).
Only the number of later queries.
The number of adjacent runs in the unsorted input.
One machine word regardless of k.

feedback.reason: Hash memory scales with retained key state.
feedback.details: In the worst case k can equal n, producing O(n) auxiliary space.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_count_only: The table must retain entries independently of q.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_runs: Hash entries are keyed by equality, not original adjacency.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant_word: A general table stores multiple entries.

items.prompt: The input must remain unchanged, but a sorting-based solution is otherwise preferred. What cost must be included?

options.text: Copying the input, adding O(n) time and O(n) auxiliary space before sorting.
The runtime supplies a free immutable sorted view.
Mutating and restoring the input has no observable risk.
Sorting becomes impossible whenever mutation is forbidden.

feedback.reason: Immutability changes the space profile of the sorting path.
feedback.details: The full comparison is hash state versus copied-and-sorted state, not hash table versus a hypothetical free in-place sort.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.free_copy: A copied or indexed representation has a cost.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mutation_hidden: Restoration can fail under errors and may violate aliasing or concurrency contracts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_required: Sorting a copy is legal if its cost is accepted.

items.prompt: A caller holds aliases to the input array and may read it concurrently. Which fact weakens an in-place sorting strategy?

options.text: Other observers can see reordered intermediate or final state.
Sorting necessarily creates hash collisions.
Aliasing makes values incomparable.
Input order is never observable through aliases.

feedback.reason: Mutation safety is broader than whether the current function later restores the array.
feedback.details: Aliasing, concurrency, exceptions, and callbacks can make temporary reordering observable.

items.prompt: A stable sorted output is required. Which memory conclusion is safe?

options.text: State the stable sorting algorithm and its buffer model; do not assume the memory of an unstable in-place sort.
Stability never changes memory requirements.
A hash map alone automatically emits stable sorted output.
Input mutation and output stability are unrelated to strategy choice.

feedback.reason: Stability constrains the sorting implementation.
feedback.details: A stable merge-based path often uses O(n) auxiliary storage, while alternatives have different time or complexity tradeoffs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.stable_free: Common stable comparison sorts use additional buffers.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_no_order: Hash iteration does not provide that contract.

items.prompt: Only a few distinct keys occur among millions of values. How does this affect the hash-memory comparison?

options.text: A frequency map may use O(k) space with k much smaller than n.
Every hash table must allocate one full entry per input occurrence.
Sorting uses zero memory in every implementation.
High duplication makes hashing illegal.

feedback.reason: The relevant hash-space parameter is retained distinct state, not necessarily input length.
feedback.details: This can make hashing memory-competitive, though bucket capacity and implementation overhead still matter.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_n_exact: Frequency aggregation normally stores one entry per distinct key.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_forbid_hash: High duplication can reduce distinct-key storage.

items.prompt: A sorting-based strategy decorates each value with its original index before sorting. What tradeoff does this introduce?

options.text: It preserves index identity but adds O(n) metadata storage or expands each sortable record.
Original indices are recovered without any stored relation.
Decoration converts the algorithm into a hash table.
Decoration prevents sorting by value.

feedback.reason: Preserving identity across reordering requires explicit metadata.
feedback.details: A lower-memory in-place sort claim may disappear once original indices must accompany every element.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_free: Sorting changes positions, so identity must be carried or reconstructed from another source.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.order_lost: Records can be sorted by value while retaining index fields.

items.prompt: Which statement best summarizes the hash-memory versus sort-mutation boundary?

options.text: Hashing commonly spends auxiliary memory to avoid reordering; sorting can reduce table memory only when its mutation and sort-buffer contracts are legal.
Hashing never allocates auxiliary state.
Sorting never changes input order.
One strategy dominates regardless of memory and mutation constraints.

feedback.reason: Memory and mutation are coupled design dimensions.
feedback.details: A fair comparison states whether input may change, whether a copy is needed, and which concrete table and sorting algorithms are used.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_winner: The preferred strategy changes with the contract.

items.prompt: Which memory components should be counted in the comparison? Select all that apply.

options.text: Hash buckets, entries, keys, and stored metadata.
Sorting recursion stack or temporary buffer.
A copy required to preserve immutable input.
The pre-existing input itself as newly allocated auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: Peak auxiliary memory includes every live structure created by the chosen strategy.
feedback.details: Input and required output should be reported separately from auxiliary space.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.original_input: Input space is not created by the algorithm.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sort_stack_buffer: The sort implementation owns this memory.

items.prompt: Which designs preserve the original input order? Select all that apply.

options.text: Scan the input while storing hash state without reordering it.
Sort a separate copy.
Create decorated records and sort those records.
Sort the original array in place and expose it afterward as unchanged.

feedback.reason: Non-mutation can be achieved by hashing or by sorting an independent representation.
feedback.details: Those alternatives have different auxiliary-space profiles.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.in_place_original: The final order differs unless explicitly restored, and temporary mutation may still be observable.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.decorate_copy: Original data and indices remain available.

items.prompt: When can sorting credibly claim the lower-auxiliary-memory path? Select all that apply.

options.text: Reordering the original input is legal.
A concrete in-place sort with bounded stack or buffer is chosen.
Original positions do not need to be retained.
A stable output requires an O(n) temporary buffer.

feedback.reason: Sorting's memory advantage depends on being able to reuse the input storage directly.
feedback.details: Stability, immutability, and index retention can remove that advantage.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.in_place_sort: The memory claim becomes algorithm-specific and defensible.

items.prompt: Which conditions can make hashing's auxiliary memory smaller than a naive O(n) estimate? Select all that apply.

options.text: Only k much smaller than n distinct keys are retained.
The algorithm stops after finding an early duplicate and releases state.
A compact bounded-domain table or bitset replaces general entries when the contract permits it.
Every input value is distinct.

feedback.reason: Space analysis should use the actual retained state and execution contract.
feedback.details: Worst-case all-distinct input still gives O(n) general hash storage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: Then k = n, maximizing retained entries.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.early_exit: Peak state may be smaller on that execution.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.bounded_domain_compact: The representation can be smaller than a generic map.

items.prompt: Arrange the decision process for the memory-versus-mutation tradeoff.

elements.text: State whether input reordering is observable and legal.
State whether original order, indices, or stable ordering must be preserved.
Model hash entries, copies, decorations, sort stack, and buffers.
Compare peak live memory only among strategies that satisfy the contract.

feedback.reason: Legality precedes optimization.
feedback.details: Peak auxiliary memory can be compared fairly only after every required representation has been included.

items.prompt: Arrange a copy-before-sort workflow that preserves the original input.

elements.text: Allocate and copy n input records.
Sort the copied representation with the selected algorithm.
Scan or query the sorted copy to produce the result.
Release the copy and any sort buffer when the result is complete.

feedback.reason: The copy is live throughout sorting and ordered consumption.
feedback.details: Its O(n) footprint must be included in peak auxiliary space.

items.prompt: Arrange a non-mutating hash workflow for duplicate or frequency analysis.

elements.text: Read each input value without reordering the sequence.
Test or update the key's stored state.
Produce the required result and release the table.

feedback.reason: Hashing preserves the input by moving derived state into an auxiliary table.
feedback.details: The table's peak size follows the number of retained keys and metadata.

items.prompt: Detect whether n hashable values contain a duplicate using a hash set and stop only after the full scan in the worst case. Select expected time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Expected set insertion and membership are O(1).

feedback.reason: The hash path avoids mutation but may retain every distinct value.
feedback.details: Expected linear time is paired with linear worst-case auxiliary storage.

items.prompt: Detect duplicates by sorting the original array in place with O(n log n) worst-case time and O(log n) stack, then scanning adjacent values. Select total time and auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: The adjacent scan is linear and does not change the sort-dominated time.
feedback.details: The lower auxiliary-space profile is valid only because in-place mutation is allowed.

items.prompt: Preserve the original array by copying n values, then run an in-place O(n log n) sort on the copy using O(log n) stack. Select total time and peak auxiliary space excluding output.
constraints: The copy remains live during sorting and scanning.

feedback.reason: The linear copy is dominated by sorting time, while the O(n) copy dominates the O(log n) sort stack in peak space.
feedback.details: This is the correct sorting-path comparison when mutation is forbidden.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Hashing typically buys non-mutating expected lookup with O(n) auxiliary state; sorting may reduce auxiliary state only when mutation is legal and the chosen sort's memory contract is explicit.
